title: Finding probabilities with sample means
description: Practice finding probabilities involving the sampling distribution of a sample mean.
topic_path: Sampling distribution of a sample mean

assessment_items.question_content:
A car manufacturer crash tests a certain model of car and measures the impact force. The test and model in question produce impact forces that are normally distributed with a mean of $30$ metric tons and a standard deviation of $1.5$ metric tons. Suppose that the manufacturer tests a random sample of $4$ cars and calculates the sample mean impact force.

**What is the probability that the mean impact force from a sample of $4$ cars $\bar x$ exceeds $30.75$ metric tons?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar{x}$. So we need to identify the shape, the mean $\mu_{\bar x}$, and the standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar x}$ of the sampling distribution in question.

###The shape of the sampling distribution

When a population is normally distributed, the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ will also be normal regardless of sample size.

Since the distribution of impact forces is normal, the sampling distribution of the sample mean impact force will also be normal.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the true population mean.

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=30$ metric tons.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=30$.

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

It's reasonable to assume independence since the sample size of $n=4$ cars is certainly less than $10\%$ of the entire population, so the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar x$ can be found by using the formula below.

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

So, the standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{1.5}{\sqrt{4}}=\dfrac{1.5}{2}=0.75$

###Calculating $P(\bar x>30.75)$

We established that $\bar x$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\bar x>30.75$:

![A normal distribution curve. The horizontal axis represents x bar, and goes from 27.75 to 32.25. The mean, or mu sub x bar, = 30. The standard deviations, or sigma sub x bar, = 0.75. The value x bar = 30.75 is marked. The area under the curve to the right of 30.75 is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/eb3416dcec4aa11fa500ec913135f07a3f212648)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 30.75 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 99 \\\\
&\mu=30 \\\\
&\sigma=0.75
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx0.1587$

###Answer

$P(\bar x > 30.75) \approx 0.16$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

choices.content: We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
A company sells eggs whose individual weights are normally distributed with a mean of $70\,\text{g}$ and a standard deviation of $2\,\text{g}$. Suppose that these eggs are sold in packages that each contain $4$ eggs that represent an [[☃ definition 1]] from the population.

**What is the probability that the mean weight of $4$ eggs in a package $\bar x$ is less than $68.5\,\text{g}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar{x}$. So we need to identify the shape, the mean $\mu_{\bar x}$, and the standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar x}$ of the sampling distribution in question.

###The shape of the sampling distribution

When a population is normally distributed, the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ will also be normal regardless of sample size.

Since the distribution of egg weights is normal, the sampling distribution of the sample mean egg weight will also be normal.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the true population mean.

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=70\,\text{g}$.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=70\,\text{g}$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

It's reasonable to assume independence since the sample size of $n=4$ eggs is certainly less than $10\%$ of the entire population, so the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar x$ can be found by using the formula below.

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

So, the standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{4}}=\dfrac{2}{2}=1\,\text{g}$

###Calculating $P(\bar x<68.5)$

We established that $\bar x$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\bar x<68.5$:

![A normal distribution curve. The horizontal axis represents x bar, and goes from 67 to 73. The mean, or mu sub x bar, = 70. The standard deviations, or sigma sub x bar, = 1. The value x bar = 68.5 is marked. The area under the curve to the left of 68.5 is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ae050244c4f934f147cc99b624c16615901b8fb0)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 68.5 \\\\
&\mu=70 \\\\
&\sigma=1
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.0668$

###Answer

$P(\bar x < 68.5) \approx 0.07$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

choices.content: We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
Individual bottles of water are filled by a machine at a factory with an amount of water that is approximately normal with a mean of $505\,\text{mL}$ and a standard deviation of $10\,\text{mL}$. A random sample of $16$ bottles is selected for a quality inspection.

**What is the probability that the mean amount of water in these $16$ bottles $\bar x$ is within $5\,\text{mL}$ of the population mean?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar{x}$. So we need to identify the shape, the mean $\mu_{\bar x}$, and the standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar x}$ of the sampling distribution in question.

###The shape of the sampling distribution

When a population is normally distributed, the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ will also be normal regardless of sample size.

Since the distribution of amounts is approximately normal, the sampling distribution of the sample mean amount will also be normal.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the true population mean.

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=505\,\text{mL}$.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=505\,\text{mL}$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

It's reasonable to assume independence since the sample size of $n=16$ bottles is certainly less than $10\%$ of the entire population, so the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar x$ can be found by using the formula below.

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

So, the standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{16}}=\dfrac{10}{4}=2.5\,\text{mL}$

###Calculating $P(500 <\bar x < 510)$

We established that $\bar x$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that the sample mean $\bar x$ is within $5\,\text{mL}$ of the the population mean:

![A normal distribution curve. The horizontal axis represents x bar, and goes from 498 to 513. The mean, or mu sub x bar, = 505. The standard deviations, or sigma sub x bar, = 2.5. The values x bar = 500 and x bar = 513 are marked. The area under the curve between the values is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bbff60413dac65f56633c90d3c6b51be8357ddc6)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 500 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 510 \\\\
&\mu=505 \\\\
&\sigma=2.5
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.9545$

###Answer

$P(500 <\bar x < 510) \approx 0.95$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

choices.content: We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
A manufacturer makes integrated circuits that each have a resistance layer with a target thickness of $200$ units. A circuit won't work well if this thickness varies too much from the target value. These thickness measurements are approximately normally distributed with a mean of $200$ units and a standard deviation of $12$ units. A random sample of $16$ measurements is selected for a quality inspection. We can assume that the measurements in the sample are independent.

**What is the probability that the mean thickness in these $16$ measurements $\bar x$ is farther than $3$ units away from the target value?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar{x}$. So we need to identify the shape, the mean $\mu_{\bar x}$, and the standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar x}$ of the sampling distribution in question.

###The shape of the sampling distribution

When a population is normally distributed, the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ will also be normal regardless of sample size.

Since the distribution of measurements is approximately normal, the sampling distribution of the sample mean measurement will also be normal.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the true population mean.

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=200$ units.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=200$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

We were told to assume independence for the $n=16$ measurements in the sample, so the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar x$ can be found by using the formula below.

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

So, the standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{12}{\sqrt{16}}=\dfrac{12}{4}=3$

###Calculating the probability

We want the probability that $\bar x$ is farther than $3$ units away from the target value of $200$. This is equivalent to the probability that $\bar x$ is greater than $203$ or less than $197$.

We established that $\bar x$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that the sample mean $\bar x$ is greater than $203$. Using symmetry, we can double that probability to include the chances of $\bar x$ being less than $197$: 

![A normal distribution curve. The horizontal axis represents x bar, and goes from 191 to 209. The mean, or mu sub x bar, = 200. The standard deviations, or sigma sub x bar, = 3. The values x bar = 197 and x bar = 203 are marked. The areas under the curve to the left of 197 and to the right of 203 are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0f87d92ee8356a9cb99dea10c2ff878bc651c45a)

Upper tail:  

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 203 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=200 \\\\
&\sigma=3
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.1587$

Both tails:  
$\begin{align}
&\approx 0.1587\times2 \\\\
&\approx 0.3173
\end{align}$

###Answer

$\approx 0.32$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

choices.content: We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
Houseflies have pretty short lifespans. Males of a certain species have lifespans that are strongly skewed to the right with a mean of $26$ days and a standard deviation of $12$ days. A biologist collects a random sample of $9$ of these male houseflies and observes them to calculate the sample mean lifespan.

**What is the probability that the mean lifespan from the sample of $9$ houseflies $\bar x$ is less than $24$ days?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar{x}$. So we need to identify the shape, the mean $\mu_{\bar x}$, and the standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar x}$ of the sampling distribution in question.

###The shape of the sampling distribution

When a population is not normally distributed, the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ depends on the sample size.

For smaller samples $(n<30)$, the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will match the shape of the parent population.

For larger samples $(n \geq 30)$, the central limit theorem tells us that the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will be approximately normal regardless of the shape of the parent population.
Since the population of lifespans is skewed to the right, and our sample size of $9$ houseflies is small $(n<30)$, the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ isn't normal.

###Answer

We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

choices.content: We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
A human resources manager keeps a record of how many years each employee at a large company has been working in their current role. The distribution of these years of experience are strongly skewed to the right with a mean of $3$ years and a standard deviation of $2$ years. Suppose we were to take a random sample of $4$ employees and calculate the sample mean for their years of experience. We can assume independence between members in the sample.

**What is the probability that the mean years of experience from the sample of $4$ employees $\bar x$ is greater than $3.5$ years?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar{x}$. So we need to identify the shape, the mean $\mu_{\bar x}$, and the standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar x}$ of the sampling distribution in question.

###The shape of the sampling distribution

When a population is not normally distributed, the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ depends on the sample size.

For smaller samples $(n<30)$, the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will match the shape of the parent population.

For larger samples $(n \geq 30)$, the central limit theorem tells us that the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will be approximately normal regardless of the shape of the parent population.
Since the population of years of experience is skewed to the right, and our sample size of $4$ employees is small $(n<30)$, the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ isn't normal.

###Answer

We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

choices.content: We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mathieu grows specialty tomatoes that are much larger than typical tomatoes. The distribution of their weights is strongly skewed to the left with a mean of $232\,\text{g}$ and a standard deviation of $12\,\text{g}$. Suppose we were to calculate the mean weight from a random sample of $16$ of Mathieu's tomatoes. We can assume independence between tomatoes in the sample.

**What is the probability that the mean weight from the sample of $16$ tomatoes $\bar x$ is within $6\,\text{g}$ of the population mean?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar{x}$. So we need to identify the shape, the mean $\mu_{\bar x}$, and the standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar x}$ of the sampling distribution in question.

###The shape of the sampling distribution

When a population is not normally distributed, the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ depends on the sample size.

For smaller samples $(n<30)$, the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will match the shape of the parent population.

For larger samples $(n \geq 30)$, the central limit theorem tells us that the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will be approximately normal regardless of the shape of the parent population.
Since the population of weights for these tomatoes is skewed to the left, and our sample size of $16$ tomatoes is small $(n<30)$, the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ isn't normal.

###Answer

We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

choices.content: We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
A pizza chain records how long it takes customers to receive their delivery orders. Suppose the distribution of these delivery times is strongly skewed to the left with a mean of $30$ minutes and a standard deviation of $10$ minutes. Management plans on calculating the mean delivery time from a random sample of $25$ orders. We can assume independence between orders in the sample.

**What is the probability that the mean delivery time from the sample of $25$ orders $\bar x$ is farther than $2$ minutes from the population mean?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar{x}$. So we need to identify the shape, the mean $\mu_{\bar x}$, and the standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar x}$ of the sampling distribution in question.

###The shape of the sampling distribution

When a population is not normally distributed, the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ depends on the sample size.

For smaller samples $(n<30)$, the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will match the shape of the parent population.

For larger samples $(n \geq 30)$, the central limit theorem tells us that the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will be approximately normal regardless of the shape of the parent population.
Since the population of delivery times is skewed to the left, and our sample size of $25$ orders is small $(n<30)$, the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ isn't normal.

###Answer

We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

choices.content: We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
Male mosquitos have pretty short lifespans. Males of a certain species have lifespans that are strongly skewed to the right with a mean of $8$ days and a standard deviation of $6$ days. A biologist collects a random sample of $36$ of these male mosquitos and observes them to calculate the sample mean lifespan.

**What is the probability that the mean lifespan from the sample of $36$ mosquitos $\bar x$ exceeds $10$ days?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar{x}$. So we need to identify the shape, the mean $\mu_{\bar x}$, and the standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar x}$ of the sampling distribution in question.

###The shape of the sampling distribution

When a population is not normally distributed, the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ depends on the sample size.

For larger samples $(n \geq 30)$, the central limit theorem tells us that the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will be approximately normal regardless of the shape of the parent population.

Since our sample size of $36$ mosquitos is large $(n>30)$, the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the true population mean.

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=8$ days.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=8$.

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

It's reasonable to assume independence since the sample size of $n=36$ mosquitos is likely less than $10\%$ of the entire population, so the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar x$ can be found by using the formula below.

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

So, the standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{6}{\sqrt{36}}=\dfrac{6}{6}=1$

###Calculating $P(\bar x>10)$

We established that $\bar x$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\bar x>10$:

![A normal distribution curve. The horizontal axis represents x bar, and goes from 5 to 11. The mean, or mu sub x bar, = 8. The standard deviations, or sigma sub x bar, = 1. The value x bar = 10 is marked. The area under the curve to the right of 10 is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/33bea4e017864779bc2cf5a94d9dd3ed08e09381)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 10 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 99 \\\\
&\mu=8 \\\\
&\sigma=1
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx0.0228$

###Answer

$P(\bar x > 10) \approx 0.02$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

choices.content: We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
A scientist studying water quality measures the lead level in parts per billion (ppb) at each of $49$ randomly chosen locations along a water line. Suppose that the lead levels across all the locations on this line are strongly skewed to the right with a mean of $17\,\text{ppb}$ and a standard deviation of $14\,\text{ppb}$. Assume that the measurements in the sample are independent.

**What is the probability that the mean lead level from the sample of $49$ measurements $\bar x$ is less than $15\,\text{ppb}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar{x}$. So we need to identify the shape, the mean $\mu_{\bar x}$, and the standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar x}$ of the sampling distribution in question.

###The shape of the sampling distribution

When a population is not normally distributed, the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ depends on the sample size.

For larger samples $(n \geq 30)$, the central limit theorem tells us that the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will be approximately normal regardless of the shape of the parent population.

Since our sample size of $49$ measurements is large $(n>30)$, the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the true population mean.

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=17\,\text{ppb}$.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=17\,\text{ppb}$.

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

We were told to assume independence for the measurements in the sample, so the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar x$ can be found by using the formula below.

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

So, the standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{14}{\sqrt{49}}=\dfrac{14}{7}=2\,\text{ppb}$

###Calculating $P(\bar x<15)$

We established that $\bar x$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\bar x<15$:

![A normal distribution curve. The horizontal axis represents x bar, and goes from 11 to 23. The mean, or mu sub x bar, = 17. The standard deviations, or sigma sub x bar, = 2. The value x bar = 15 is marked. The area under the curve to the left of 15 is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f9fb0d71e035435b7d99241d939f4deb76fbb082)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 15 \\\\
&\mu=17 \\\\
&\sigma=2
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx0.1587$

###Answer

$P(\bar x < 15) \approx 0.16$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

choices.content: We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
A pizza chain monitors the total weight of pepperoni that goes on its deluxe pepperoni pizzas to make sure customers are satisfied and product isn't being wasted. Suppose that for pizzas in this population, these weights are strongly skewed to the left with a mean of $250\,\text{g}$ and a standard deviation of $8\,\text{g}$. Management takes a random sample of $64$ of these pizzas and calculates the mean weight of the pepperoni on the pizzas.  Assume that the pizzas in the sample are independent.

**What is the probability that the mean weight of the pepperoni from the sample of $64$ pizzas $\bar x$ is within $1\,\text{g}$ of the true mean?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar{x}$. So we need to identify the shape, the mean $\mu_{\bar x}$, and the standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar x}$ of the sampling distribution in question.

###The shape of the sampling distribution

When a population is not normally distributed, the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ depends on the sample size.

For larger samples $(n \geq 30)$, the central limit theorem tells us that the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will be approximately normal regardless of the shape of the parent population.

Since our sample size of $64$ pizzas is large $(n>30)$, the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the true population mean.

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=250\,\text{g}$.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=250\,\text{g}$.

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

We were told to assume independence for the pizzas in the sample, so the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar x$ can be found by using the formula below.

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

So, the standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{8}{\sqrt{64}}=\dfrac{8}{8}=1\,\text{g}$

###Calculating $P(249 <\bar x < 251)$

We established that $\bar x$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that the sample mean $\bar x$ is within $1\,\text{g}$ of the population mean of $250\,\text{g}$:

![A normal distribution curve. The horizontal axis represents x bar, and goes from 247 to 253. The mean, or mu sub x bar, = 250. The standard deviations, or sigma sub x bar, = 1. The values x bar = 249 and x bar = 251 are marked. The area under the curve between the values is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/db84369a44ccc879627ca6de5357d49f2495b2ba)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 249 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 251 \\\\
&\mu=250 \\\\
&\sigma=1
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.6827$

###Answer

$P(249 <\bar x < 251) \approx 0.68$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

choices.content: We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.

assessment_items.question_content:
A large sleep study involving over $5{,}000$ American teenagers examined how many hours the participants slept on the weekend. The nightly sleep times were distinctly non-normal with a mean of $10$ hours and a standard deviation of $2$ hours. Suppose we take a random sample of $100$ nightly sleep times from this population. We can assume that the times in the sample are independent.

**What is the probability that the mean of these $100$ sleep times $\bar x$ is farther than $0.4$ hours away from the population mean?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar{x}$. So we need to identify the shape, the mean $\mu_{\bar x}$, and the standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar x}$ of the sampling distribution in question.

###The shape of the sampling distribution

When a population is not normally distributed, the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ depends on the sample size.

For larger samples $(n \geq 30)$, the central limit theorem tells us that the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ will be approximately normal regardless of the shape of the parent population.

Since our sample size of $100$ sleep times is large $(n>30)$, the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ is approximately normal.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the true population mean.

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=10$ hours.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=10$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

We were told to assume independence for the $n=100$ times in the sample, so the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar x$ can be found by using the formula below.

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

So, the standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{100}}=\dfrac{2}{10}=0.2$

###Calculating the probability

We want the probability that $\bar x$ is farther than $0.4$ hours away from the population mean of $10$ hours. This is equivalent to the probability that $\bar x$ is greater than $10.4$ or less than $9.6$.

We established that $\bar x$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that the sample mean $\bar x$ is greater than $10.4$. Using symmetry, we can double that probability to include the chances of $\bar x$ being less than $9.6$: 

![A normal distribution curve. The horizontal axis represents x bar, and goes from 9.4 to 10.6. The mean, or mu sub x bar, = 10. The standard deviations, or sigma sub x bar, = 0.2. The values x bar = 9.6 and x bar = 10.4 are marked. The areas under the curve to the left of 9.6 and to the right of 10.4 are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0f544ec69a91cedf3c1e7e3e55878984e167ba7c)

Upper tail:  

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 10.4 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=10 \\\\
&\sigma=0.2
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.02275$

Both tails:  
$\begin{align}

choices.content: We cannot calculate this probability because the sampling distribution is not normal.
